# Sex Test: Inside the IOC's Fifty-Year Quest to Decide Who Counts as a Woman

> A work of narrative non-fiction by Emilie Filion-Donato, sociologist and MA graduate of the Université de Montréal.

## What this book is

*Sex Test* reconstructs the birth of Olympic gender testing from the inside, drawing on seventeen sets of internal IOC Medical Commission meeting minutes held in the IOC's restricted archive in Lausanne, Switzerland — documents that have never before been subject to systematic scholarly analysis.

From 1967 to 1972, the IOC Medical Commission built an entire bureaucratic apparatus to determine who counted as a woman in sport. The science they chose — the Barr body chromosomal test — was contested from the moment it was proposed. It was abandoned in 1999 without acknowledgment of failure. The same fundamental question is still being asked today, with the same inability to resolve it, in the cases of Caster Semenya and others.

## Author

**Emilie Filion-Donato** is a sociologist specializing in the governance of gender in sport. She holds an M.Sc. in Sociology from the Université de Montréal, where her archival research on the IOC Medical Commission was conducted under academic supervision. She is based in Montréal, Québec, Canada.

Contact: emiliefiliondonato.1@gmail.com

## Key research findings

- The IOC Medical Commission held its first gender verification meeting in Lausanne in September 1967.
- Ethical objections were raised at the very first meeting and consistently dismissed.
- Six distinct discourse categories structured how the Commission spoke about gender: biological essentialism, competitive fairness, medical authority, administrative efficiency, Cold War anxiety, and the protection of femininity.
- The Barr body test failed on its own scientific terms: it misclassified women with androgen insensitivity syndrome and other intersex conditions.
- The shift from Barr body testing (1968–1991) to SRY gene / PCR testing (1992–1999) was framed as scientific progress but replicated the same logic and the same failures.
- Mandatory testing was quietly abandoned in 1999. No athlete was ever formally notified that a test had been wrong about them.
- The cases of María José Martínez-Patiño (1985) and Caster Semenya (2009–present) bookend the arc of a policy that never worked.

## Comparable titles

- *The Immortal Life of Henrietta Lacks* — Rebecca Skloot
- *Invisible Women* — Caroline Criado Perez
- *The Sports Gene* — David Epstein
- *Testosterone Rex* — Cordelia Fine

## Target audience

General readers interested in sports history, feminist science studies, gender politics, Olympic history, and narrative non-fiction. Academic readers in sociology, science and technology studies (STS), and gender studies.

## Status

Book proposal complete. Seeking literary representation. Website: https://emiliefiliondonato.com/
